---
title: "Assignment 6: Introduction to Metagenomics"
author: Arturo Torres Ortiz
date: 04/09/2026
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    theme: cosmo
    highlight-style: github
  pdf:
    include-in-header: ../../header.tex
    callout-icon: false
    callout-appearance: default
    geometry:
      - top=30mm
      - left=20mm
---

For this assignment, we will perform a microbiome composition analysis using shotgun sequencing metagenomics. We will use data generated from human fecal samples to compare the gut microbiota profiles of a healthy adult at baseline (Day 0) against their microbial profile immediately following a 4-day intervention with a cocktail of three last-resort antibiotics (Day 4).

The files used for the assignment follow the sample name structure: subject_timepoint_readpair.fq.gz. For instance, ERAS6_Dag4opt_wHAXPI002161-78.clean.pair.rmhost.1.fq.gz is subject 6, timepoint 4, forward read.

The original reference paper for this dataset:

* Palleja, A., Mikkelsen, K.H., Forslund, S.K. et al. Recovery of gut microbiota of healthy adults following antibiotic exposure. Nat Microbiol 3, 1255–1265 (2018). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41564-018-0257-9

The authors utilized a deep shotgun metagenomic sequencing strategy to observe the effects of antibiotics in the gut microbiota in healthy individuals, allowing for high-resolution taxonomic profiling and the tracking of antibiotic resistance genes (the resistome) over a 6-month period.

We will process the raw metagenomic reads to identify differentially abundant microbial taxa and detect shifts in the resistome linked to antibiotic exposure.

# Removing human reads

To remove human reads, we simply map reads to the human reference genome and take extract unmapped reads.

```sh
bwa mem 
<reference_genome>
<input_1.fq.gz> <input_2.fq.gz> | 
samtools view
-@ <n_threads>
-f12 -F256 - |
samtools sort
-@ <n_threads> -n -o unmapped.bam

samtools fastq -1 paired1.fq -2 paired2.fq -0 /dev/null -s /dev/null -n unmapped.bam
```

# Removing low complexity reads

Highly conserved regions can cause missassignments during taxonomic classification. To solve this, we can remove regions in the genome with low entropy. We will use BBduck for this.

```sh
bbduk.sh
int=f
in=stdin.fq
out=stdout.fq
entropy=0.6
entropywindow=50
entropyk=5 |
gzip > output.fq.gz
```

# Read level taxonomic assignment using MetaPhlAn

A tool that is commonly used for taxonomic annotation of metagenomic sequences is MetaPhlAn, which uses a database of marker genes. You can run the latest version of MetaPhlAn directly on your reads as:

```sh
 conda activate /home1/atortiz/miniforge3/envs/mpa

metaphlan
<fwd.fastq.gz>,<rev.fastq.gz>
-t rel_ab_w_read_stats # Add absolute abundance
--bowtie2out <aln_out.bowtie2.bz2> # Alignment file
--input_type fastq
--nproc $SLURM_CPUS_PER_TASK
-o <out.mpa> # Output file
```

### Task 1: MetaPhlan output.
::: {.callout-note}
Look at the output generated by MetaPhlan and answer the following questions:

1. What are the rows in the output file? **(5pts)**
1. What are the columns in the output file? **(5pts)**
2. What does k__, p__, c__, o__, f__, g__ and s__ mean? **(5pts)**
3. Look at the relative abundance column. What does each of k__, p__, c__ etc. must sum up to? **(5pts)**
:::


Once we all have all the samples processed, we will merged them into a table. We will only keep taxa at the species level.

Metaphlan includes a script called `merge_metaphlan_tables.py` to do exactly this. We will use a modified version that keeps the absolute abundance instead of relative abundance.

I have already done this for you but here's the code for reference:

```sh
merging_script=/project/msalomon_1385/TRGN_516/microbiome/merge_metaphlan4_tables_abs.py
in_files=/project/msalomon_1385/TRGN_516/microbiome/metaphlan

$merging_script $in_files/*mpa | grep -v "#" | grep "k__Bacteria" | grep "s__" | grep -v "t__" > $in_files/all_merged.txt
```


### Task 2: MetaPhlan combined output.
::: {.callout-note}
Look at the merged output generated and answer the following questions:

1. What are the rows in the merged output file? **(5pts)**
1. What are the columns in the merged output file? **(5pts)**
:::

We can quickly get the sample names for the header in a bit of a hacky way:

```sh
for files in $in_files/*mpa; do echo \'$(basename $files | cut -d'_' -f1)_$(basename $files | cut -d_ -f2)\' | sed 's/Dag//g' | sed 's/.mpa//g' | sed 's/DAG//g' | sed 's/opt//g' | sed 's/ERAS/sample/' | tr '\n', ','; done
```

# Taxonomic analysis in R

We will continue the rest of the taxonomic analysis in R.

Load the libraries and the data:

```r
library(phyloseq)
library(vegan)
library(ggplot2)
library(taxonomizr)
library(GUniFrac)

data=read.table("/project/msalomon_1385/TRGN_516/microbiome/metaphlan", header = F)
```

```r
data$V1 = sapply(data$V1, function(x) rev(strsplit(x, '[|]')[[1]])[1])
data$V1 = substring(data$V1, 4)

colnames(data) = c('taxa', 'sample10_0','sample10_180','sample10_42','sample10_8','sample2_0',
                   'sample2_42','sample2_4','sample2_8','sample4_0','sample4_180','sample4_42',
                   'sample4_4','sample4_8','sample6_0','sample6_180','sample6_42','sample6_4',
                   'sample6_8','sample7_0','sample7_180','sample7_42','sample7_4','sample7_8',
                   'sample8_0','sample8_180','sample8_42','sample8_8','sample9_0','sample9_180',
                   'sample9_42','sample9_4','sample9_8')
```

Create taxonomic table

```r
otu_table = as.matrix(data[,-1])
rownames(otu_table) = data$taxa
```

Remove low frequency (rare) species:

```r
otu_table = otu_table[apply(otu_table, 1, function(x) any(x >= 100)),]
otu_table = otu_table[apply(otu_table, 1, function(x) length(x[x != 0])>=2),]
```

## Rarefaction

An important step in analyzing sequencing data is rarefaction. Rarefaction involves randomly subsetting samples so that all samples have even sequencing depth.


### Task 3: Rarefaction.
::: {.callout-note}
1. Why is important to have even sequencing depth? **(10pts)**
:::


To rarefy our dataset, we must first visualize the rarefaction curve of our samples using the `vegan` package. To do this, we need to create a dataframe that vegan can work with from our OTU table.

```r
rarecurve(as.data.frame(t(otu_table)), step=100,cex=0.5,label=TRUE)
```


### Task 4: Rarefaction curve.
::: {.callout-note}
1. Describe the rarefaction plot. What does the x-axis mean? What does the y-axis mean? What's the main conclusion you get out of the plot? **(10pts)**
2. Why is this plot important? **(10pts)**
3. The curve seems to plateau. Is this a good or a bad thing? Why? **(10pts)**
:::

Based on the rarefaction curve, choose a number to rarefy to and use the package `GUniFrac`:


### Task 5: Rarefaction number.
::: {.callout-note}
1. What number have you chosen for the rarefaction? **(5pts)**
2. Is this the only number you could have chosen? Could you go lower? Could you go higher? **(5pts)**
:::

```r
n_depth=<n>
otu_table = Rarefy(t(otu_table), depth = n_depth)
otu_table = t(otu_table$otu.tab.rff)
```


### Task 6: Rarefaction curve.
::: {.callout-note}
1. Repeat the rarefaction curve with your rarefied table. Explain the differences between the curves before and after rarefaction  **(10pts)**
:::

## Alpha diversity

Alpha diversity measures diversity within a sample.

### Richness

Richness measures the number of distinct species within a sample. We can do this within R:

```r
sp_rich = apply(otu_table, 2, function(x) length(x[x != 0]))
```

Within the `vegan` package you can also use:

```r
sp_rich = specnumber(t(otu_table))
```


### Task 7: Species richness.
::: {.callout-note}
1. Plot a boxplot of the richness per time point (0, 4, 8, 42 and 180). X-axis will be each timepoint while y-axis will show richness.  **(10pts)**
2. What are the effects of antibiotic usage on the gut microbiome richness given this plot? **(10pts)**
:::


### Other measures of diversity

There are multiple diversity measures. Some of them will take into account the richness, and others both the richness and the eveness.

```r
# Shannon diversity
shannon_div = vegan::diversity(t(otu_table), index = "shannon")

# Simpson diversity
simpson_div = vegan::diversity(t(otu_table), index = "simpson")

# Fisher diversity
simpson_div = vegan::fisher.alpha(t(otu_table))
```

### Analyze differences in alpha diversity

The Mann-Whitney U Test, also called Wilcoxon Rank Sum Test, is a non-parametric statistical test that is used to compare two groups, as we have here in our dataset. Being a non-parametric test, it does not assume normalcy in our data (unlike Student's test). The test assesses if the two groups are from the same population, i.e. whether they are equal.

We will do it for the species richness.

```r
sp_rich = apply(otu_table, 2, function(x) length(x[x != 0]))
df = data.frame('richness' = as.numeric(sp_rich), 'time' = trimws(gsub(".*_","",names(sp_rich))))
df$time = factor(df$time, levels = c(0, 4, 8, 42, 180))
```

We can only compare two groups using the Mann-Whitney U Test:

```r
m1 = wilcox.test(as.numeric(richness) ~ time, data = df[df$time %in% c(0, 4),])
```

To compare more than two groups,we are going to employ the Kruskal-Wallis test, which is a further extension of the Mann-Whiteny U test for three or more groups. Again, we want to find out if the sample groups are representative of different populations, and therefore different from each other.

```r
m2 = kruskal.test(as.numeric(richness) ~ time, data = df)
```

Using the Kruskal Test, we can only go so far as to say that there are differences between the groups. However, we don't know exactly which groups are different. And, because we are comparing more than 2 groups, we must incorporate multiple test correction as well.Because the Kruskal-Wallis test uses a rank-sum approach, it is appropriate to use a post-hoc test which preserves these ranks, such as Dunn's test (but an alternative could be the Conover-Iman test).

Instead of evaluating whether any of the groups are different, Dunn's test uses pairwise comparisons to evaluate which groups might be significantly different from each other. And, we will use the Benjamini-Hochberg multiple test correction method to mitigate the false-discovery rate that is inherent to a series of pairwise tests.

```r
m3 = rstatix::dunn_test(data = df, richness ~ time, p.adjust.method = "BH")
```


### Task 8: Kruskal test.
::: {.callout-note}
1. What groups are significantly different from each other? **(10pts)**
:::


## Beta diversity

Beta diversity measures diversity between samples.

First, let's transform the abundances into relative abundances. This is not neccesarry with the functions we will use, but other functions may require it:

```r
otu_rel = apply(otu_table, 2, function(x) x/sum(x))
```

Then we will calculate the Bray-Curtis dissimilarity:

```r
bray_dist = as.matrix(vegdist(t(otu_rel), method = "bray"))
times =  sapply(colnames(bray_dist), function(x) strsplit(x, '_')[[1]][2])
times = factor(times, levels = sort(unique(times)))
```

Now that we have the raw distance data, we can do a permutational ANOVA (PERMANOVA) to see if there are significant differences. The PERMANOVA is used to analyze multivariate data that uses a dissimilarity metric. It is a consensus-based approach that uses the whole dataset and random subsets of the data to calculate a test statistic. In R, this can be done with the adonis2() function from the vegan package. PERMANOVA and Adonis tend to be used interchangeably to talk about the same test. To run our Adonis, use the following code:

```r
adonis=adonis2(as.dist(bray_dist) ~ times)
```

Next, we will create an ordination and plot it to first visualize the differences between our groups. 

```r
library("ade4")
library("vegan")

meta = metaMDS(bray_dist, k=2) # k=2 to keep only 2 ordinations

s.class(
  meta$points, col = RColorBrewer::brewer.pal(length(levels(times)), 'Set1'), cpoint = 2, fac = times)
```

We can again conduct our PERMANOVA to compare all the groups in a pairwise manner.

```r
devtools::install_github("pmartinezarbizu/pairwiseAdonis/pairwiseAdonis")
library('pairwiseAdonis')

pairwise=pairwise.adonis(as.dist(bray_dist), times, p.adjust.m = 'BH')
```


### Task 9: Beta diversity.
::: {.callout-note}
1. Look at the MDS plot. What conclusion do you get visually? **(10pts)**
2. Analuze the `pairwise` object from the pairwise adonis result. What groups are significantly different? **(5pts)**
:::

<!-- # Metagenomic assembly

The first step in the assembly of MAGs is the assembly of the shorter reads from sequencing into contigs. A contig is a contiguous sequence assembled from a set of sequence fragments, and there are different bioinformatic methods for assembling our reads into contigs.

Common assemblers for metagenomic Illumina data include `MEGAHIT` or `Spades` (previously metaSpades).

For the Illumina data, we will perform genome assembly using the Spades assembler. Short-read assemblies rely on dividing the short-reads into k-mers. Choosing k-mer and other parameters can be tricky and many assemblers (including spades) will automatically select values for its parameters based on the input data. Also, Spades allows the user to select multiple k-mer sizes to maximise the chance of finding an ideal assembly. For this data, I propose using k-mer sizes of 21, 33, and 55bp. You can also use different k-mers or let Spades decide by not using the `-k` flag.

```sh
kmers=21,33,55

spades.py
-o <out_dir> # Output directory
-1 <fwd.fastq> # Forward reads
-2 <rev.fastq> # Reverse reads
--meta # Metagenomics mode
-t $SLURM_CPUS_PER_TASK # Multithreading
-k $kmers # Kmer size (skip for automatic selection)
```

Spades outputs contigs with consistent headers that include information regarding contig length and contig coverage (number of reads included in the contig). An example of contig header for a contig of 100,000bp in length and 100x coverage:

```

>NODE_1_length_100000_cov_100
```

This makes it very handy for filtering poor quality contigs using only the information from the header. You can filter using the following command:

```sh
seqkit fx2tab $contigs | 
csvtk mutate -H -t -f 1 -p "cov_(.+)" | 
csvtk mutate -H -t -f 1 -p "length_([0-9]+)" | 
awk -F "\t" '$4>=10 && $5>=1000' | # Only keep contigs with coverage higher or equal than 10 reads and length of at least 1000bp
seqkit tab2fx > $out_dir/$sample.filt_contigs.fa
```

You can use MEGAHIT easily as well:

```sh
megahit
-1 <fwd.fastq> # Forward reads
-2 <rev.fastq> # Reverse reads
--min-contig-len <n>
--num-cpu-threads $SLURM_CPUS_PER_TASK # Multithreading
--presets meta-large
--memory 0.8
-o <out_dir>
--verbose
```

You can use seqkit to remove short contigs (not needed as we filtered them in the previous step for Megahit):

```sh
seqkit seq -m 1500 scaffolds.fasta > out.fasta
```


# Functional analysis

## Comprehensive Antibiotic Resistance Database (CARD) Resistance Gene Identifier (RGI)

```sh
conda create --name rgi rgi
conda activate rgi
```

rgi will not work in the login node (and you shouldn't run code there anyways), so get an interactive working node using `salloc`.

Often when we run tools like this, we'll need to download and setup the database before we can use it.

Move to the database directory

```sh
cd /project/msalomon_1385/TRGN_516/microbiome/card_data
```

Download the main database.
```sh
wget https://card.mcmaster.ca/latest/data --no-check-certificate
tar -xvf data ./card.json
```

Load the database. You can check the version to see that is properly loaded

```sh
rgi load --card_json ./card.json --local
rgi database --version --local
```

Now we'll get another part of the database that uses WildCARD and expands CARD by including computationally predicted AMR genes and their variants,

```sh
wget -O wildcard_data.tar.bz2 https://card.mcmaster.ca/latest/variants
mkdir -p wildcard
tar -xjf wildcard_data.tar.bz2 -C wildcard
gunzip wildcard/*.gz
```

Now we'll get the annotations:

```sh
rgi card_annotation -i localDB/card.json > card_annotation.log 2>&1
rgi wildcard_annotation -i wildcard --card_json localDB/card.json -v 4.0.0 > wildcard_annotation.log 2>&1
```

Load the database:

```sh
rgi load \
  --card_json localDB/card.json \
  --debug --local \
  --card_annotation card_database_v4.0.0.fasta \
  --wildcard_annotation wildcard_database_v4.0.0.fasta \
  --wildcard_index wildcard/index-for-model-sequences.txt \
  --wildcard_version 4.0.0 \
  --amr_kmers wildcard/all_amr_61mers.txt \
  --kmer_database wildcard/61_kmer_db.json \
  --kmer_size 61
```

I have done up to this point for you. You can take it from here!

You have two options:

1. You can `cd` into the database directory I created and run the `rgi main` commands from there, making sure the paths to input and output are correct.
2. Re-run the `rgi load` command from your working directory, putting the full path to each of the files the `rgi load` needs (eg: /project/msalomon_1385/TRGN_516/microbiome/card_data/localDB/card.json instead of localDB/card.json). Then you run the `rgi main` command from your working directory.

Make sure you are in the correct directory. For this markdown I will put the database directory (option 1 above). If you followed option 2, then `cd` into your working directory.

```sh
cd /project/msalomon_1385/TRGN_516/microbiome/card_data
```

Run CARD `rgi` in your contigs.

**Make sure to add enough memory, for me it worked with 100Gb:**

```sh
rgi main # Main rgi function
-i <assembly.fasta> # Input assembly file (Megahit or Spades output)
-o <out_dir/prefix> # Output directory and output prefix
-t contig # Whether we have contigs or proteins
-a DIAMOND # Alignment tool to use
-n  $SLURM_CPUS_PER_TASK # Multithreading
--include_loose # Include loose hits in addition to strict and perfect hits
--local # Use local DB
--clean # Remove temporary files
```

We can run a quick heatmap for visualization:

```sh
rgi heatmap --input <card_dirt> --output card_heatmap
```

## Analyze differences in functional categories

Within R, let's analyze the results of the CARD functional analysis. Our analysis will focus on finding if there're differences in the number of genes between time points. Instead of the genes themselves, we will focus on functional categories. We could analyze the frequency of those genes, rather than the number, by mapping reads back to the contigs. But to simplify the analysis, let's just use the number of reads.

So the question we will answer is: do functional categories change between time points?

First, let's load the data:

```r
library(dplyr)

in_dir = "/project/msalomon_1385/TRGN_516/microbiome/plot_class/time_out"

files = list.files(path = in_dir, pattern = '*.txt', full.names = T)

res = c()
for (file in files){
  print(file)
  patient = gsub("^\\d+_(.*?)\\.txt$", '\\1', basename(file))
  time = strsplit(basename(file), '_')[[1]][1]
  df = as.data.frame(fread(file, select = c('Cut_Off', 'Best_Hit_ARO', 'Drug Class', 'Resistance Mechanism', 'AMR Gene Family', 'Antibiotic')))
  res = rbind(res, cbind(df[df$Cut_Off != "Loose",], time, patient))
}
```

Now we need to summarize and get the counts for each functional category by sample and timepoint. I have chosen the package `dplyr` to do this:

```r
result_df <- res %>%
  group_by(patient, time, `Resistance Mechanism`) %>%
  summarise(count = n(), .groups = 'drop') %>% as.data.frame()
```

There's a few entries with multiple mechanisms. These are separated by semicolons. The numbers for these entries are very low, so for this assignment we will remove them.

```r
result_df = result_df[!grepl(';', result_df$`Resistance Mechanism`),]
```

Not all sample-time combinations have all mechanisms, which we should indicate with a count of 0 (rather than that combination being absent). We will add those missing rows:

```r
# 1. Get all unique patient and time combinations and all mechanisms
all_combinations = result_df %>% distinct(patient, time)
all_mechanisms = unique(result_df$`Resistance Mechanism`)

# 2. Loop over all fill it up
for (i in all_mechanisms){
  for (j in 1:nrow(all_combinations)){
    if(!i %in% result_df$`Resistance Mechanism`[result_df$patient == all_combinations$patient[j] & result_df$time == all_combinations$time[j]]){
      result_df = rbind(result_df, c(all_combinations$patient[j], all_combinations$time[j], i, 0))
    }
  }
}

###################
# 2.1 In case you don't like loops, you could also do it with a combination of dplyr and tidyr. I do find loop very intuitive but they are not for everyone! You don't need to run this if you ran the loop before
library(tidyr)

# Create a complete grid of all combinations
complete_grid = expand_grid(all_combinations, `Resistance Mechanism` = all_mechanisms)

result_df = complete_grid %>%
  left_join(result_df, by = c("patient", "time", "Resistance Mechanism")) %>%
  mutate(count = coalesce(count, 0)) %>% as.data.frame()
###################
```

Let's make sure the variables are what we want them to be: numeric for "count" and factor for "time":

```r
result_df$count = as.numeric(result_df$count)
result_df$time = factor(result_df$time, levels = c("0", "4", "8", "42", '180'))
```

Now we can run the model. Let's run a Poisson model (which models count data) for each mechanism separately.

```r
model_results <- list()
for (mechanism in all_mechanisms) {
  subset_data = result_df[result_df$`Resistance Mechanism` %in% mechanism,]
    
  # Fit a Poisson GLM
  model = glm(count ~ time, family = poisson(link = "log"), data = subset_data)
  
  # Tidy the model output
  tidy_model <- broom::tidy(model) %>%
    mutate(mechanism = mechanism)
  
  model_results[[mechanism]] = tidy_model
}

# Combine the results for all mechanisms
model_results = do.call(rbind, model_results)

model_results = model_results %>%
  filter(term != "(Intercept)") %>%
  select(mechanism, term, estimate, std.error, p.value) %>%
  arrange(mechanism, term) %>% as.data.frame()
```

:::info
**Task 10: Gene mechanisms.**

1. Interpret the model_results object and the p-values. Is there any mechanism that stands out? Are the estimated effects positive or negative? What does that mean? **(10pts)**
:::

## Analyze differences in gene content

Let's repeat the analysis, but this time we will look at the genes rather than the functional categories. Our final goal is to get a heatmap:

```r
# Let's use the same variable as before, but now we select the Best_Hit_ARO column
result_df <- res %>%
  group_by(patient, time, `Best_Hit_ARO`) %>%
  summarise(count = n(), .groups = 'drop') %>% as.data.frame()

# Let's add those sample-time combinations that don't have any gene present
all_combinations = result_df %>% distinct(patient, time)
all_genes = unique(result_df$Best_Hit_ARO)

for (i in all_genes){
  for (j in 1:nrow(all_combinations)){
    if(!i %in% result_df$Best_Hit_ARO[result_df$patient == all_combinations$patient[j] & result_df$time == all_combinations$time[j]]){
      result_df = rbind(result_df, c(all_combinations$patient[j], all_combinations$time[j], i, 0))
    }
  }
}

# Let's put back the time_sample names
result_df <- result_df %>%
  mutate(time_patient = paste0(time, "_", patient))

# Make the count binary - gene presence/absence
result_df$count = ifelse(result_df$count >= 1, 1, 0)
```

Now we can reformat our dataframe to get a matrix. 

```r
library(tidyr)

matrix_df <- result_df %>%
  pivot_wider(id_cols = Best_Hit_ARO,
              names_from = time_patient,
              values_from = count,
              values_fill = 0)

# Convert the pivoted data frame to a matrix
output_matrix <- as.matrix(matrix_df[, -1]) # Exclude the Best_Hit_ARO column (This will become the rownames)

# Set the row names of the matrix
rownames(output_matrix) <- matrix_df$Best_Hit_ARO

# Let's order by time
output_matrix = output_matrix[,order(sapply(colnames(output_matrix), function(x) as.numeric(strsplit(x, '_')[[1]][1])))]
```

Now we can plot the heatmap. First, let's not reorder and keep the matrix's original row and column order.

```r
heatmap(output_matrix, scale = "none", Rowv = NA, Colv = NA)
```
Light color is absence of a gene, while the darker red is presence of a gene.

The heatmap also includes a clustering function based on pairwise distances. It will reorganize the samples and the genes based on this clustering. Let's check it out and visualize the result.

```r
heatmap(output_matrix, scale = "none")
```

:::info
**Task 11: Gene presence/absence.**

Choose one of the two heatmaps and add it to the assignment.

1. Look at the heatmaps we produced. Is there any sample clustering that stands out? Is there any group of sample-times that seem to cluster together? **(10pts)**

Choose a gene from that cluster. Find the gene in the Kegg database (https://www.genome.jp/kegg/genes.html) and the CARD database (https://card.mcmaster.ca/)

2. Why do you choose that gene? How is that gene distributed across time-points? **(10pts)**
3. What is the function of that gene? What is the resistance mechanism? **(10pts)**
:::
 -->
